one_line_takeaway: POLAR-Bench introduces a two-agent adversarial benchmark that crosses graded privacy-policy difficulty and graded attack strategy into a deterministic 5x5 privacy-utility diagnostic surface, finding that frontier models withhold >99% of protected attributes while many locally-deployable 1-30B open-weight models -- the class users actually run as trusted on-device agents -- leak over half, and that multi-turn/yes-no narrowing attacks (not direct requests or prompt injection) are the most effective at extracting protected information.
key_finding: Frontier models withhold over 99% of protected attributes while maintaining high utility (GLM-5.1 tops the overall ranking at 94.6, ahead of GPT-5.4 at 92.6, Gemma-4-31B at 91.4, DeepSeek-V3.1 at 91.0, Gemma-4-E4B at 90.9), whereas smaller open-weight models in the 1-30B range -- the class most commonly run as a user's own trusted on-device/private-inference agent -- score substantially worse, with the weakest (e.g. SmolLM3-3B, privacy 46.32; Apertus models) leaking over half of protected attributes. The privacy-utility trade-off is not monotonic: some mid-tier models (GLM-4.7-Flash, Kimi-K2.5, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, GPT-OSS-20B/120B) achieve near-maximal privacy but only moderate/low utility (over-conservative), while others (SmolLM3-3B, Ministral-3-3B) favor utility at the cost of privacy. Model size is not a reliable predictor within a family: Apertus-70B beats Apertus-8B on utility but its privacy drops from 65.66 to 57.78; the Gemma-4 family shows the opposite pattern; Ministral privacy is non-monotonic (62.03 → 68.04 → 58.99 across 3B/8B/14B). Reasoning capability (proxied by GPQA Diamond across 20 models) correlates strongly with privacy (Pearson r=0.752 [0.500,0.903], Spearman ρ=0.848 [0.559,0.966], both p<0.001) but is weakly negatively associated with utility (r=-0.291 [-0.648,0.083], ρ=-0.156 [-0.609,0.331], both CIs straddling zero). Attack strategy matters far more than privacy-policy dimension: S2 (yes/no narrowing) and S5 (multi-turn progressive) are the most privacy-threatening -- S2 yields the lowest average privacy score and largest cross-model variance, while S5 preserves high utility while still substantially reducing privacy -- whereas direct single-turn (S1) and prompt-injection (S4) attacks are comparatively easier to defend against and produce more consistent behavior across models. Applying the PrivacyChecker inference-time defense on Ministral-3-3B, Apertus-70B, and SmolLM3-3B raised privacy scores in all three cases (+27.52, +5.89, +10.20 respectively) with small utility effects (-2.72, +1.54, +0.40), showing the benchmark is sensitive to a real intervention. Sensitivity checks: choice of third-party model E has minimal effect on a strong T (Llama-3.3-70B's overall score varies only 0.2pp across three different E models) but a larger effect on a weaker T (3.9pp swing in privacy score for Apertus-70B across three E models); domain-level rankings are broadly stable (Spearman correlations of domain-vs-overall ranking range 0.893-0.982, Kendall's W=0.915).
core_contribution: POLAR-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark for privacy-utility trade-offs in LLM agents that acts as a trusted party on a user's behalf. A trusted model T, given a source document, a user privacy policy, and a task, converses with a third-party model E that adversarially probes for both task-relevant and protected attributes. Every attribute in the source document is typed before natural-language rendering, so privacy leakage and task utility are both scored deterministically by regex/set-membership matching against the transcript, with no LLM judge in the scoring loop. The benchmark's core novelty (vs. prior privacy benchmarks like AirGapAgent, ConfAIde, PrivacyLens, AGENTDAM, and AgentLeak) is crossing two orthogonal graded axes -- privacy policy dimension (P1 explicit field constraints through P5 conflicting objectives) and attack strategy (S1 direct single-turn through S5 multi-turn progressive elicitation) -- into a 5x5 diagnostic surface per model, evaluated jointly on privacy AND utility rather than privacy alone. The dataset spans 10 domains (medical, recruitment, finance, education, customer support, legal, insurance, housing, travel, cybersecurity) with 7,852 total samples (733-1000 per domain), generated via a two-stage pipeline (symbolic profile sampling, then LLM natural-language rendering) with an explicit constraint that task instructions and privacy policies are decoupled (Ti ⊥ Pi) so the trusted model cannot rely on shortcut cues.
threat_model: Two-agent adversarial setting inherited from AirGapAgent: a trusted model T holds a private source document (containing task-relevant, protected, and irrelevant/noise attributes) plus a user-supplied privacy policy and task instruction, and converses with an externally-prompted third-party model E that is adversarially instructed to elicit both task-relevant and protected attributes. The paper does not assume E has any access beyond the conversation itself (no model weights, no side channels) -- the attack surface is purely conversational/prompt-based, ranging from a single direct request (S1) to binary yes/no narrowing (S2), role confusion/authority framing (S3), prompt injection via authority or system/debug pretenses (S4), and multi-turn progressive elicitation across 3-6 turns (S5). The defender's obligation is to follow the user's privacy policy under this adversarial pressure while still sharing enough to complete the task; the policy itself is varied in difficulty from explicit field-name rules (P1) to abstract/conflicting objectives (P5).
limitations: Stated explicitly by the authors: (1) samples are generated from structured synthetic profiles rather than real user logs, so external validity depends on how faithfully rendered documents track deployed text; (2) the P1-P5 policy and S1-S5 attack difficulty gradings are the authors' own design and are not validated against human-reviewer judgments of difficulty ordering; (3) coverage is English-only, excluding multilingual privacy norms and culturally variable disclosure expectations; (4) a single fixed model (Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct) generates every attacker prompt Q_i in the main results, so the reported attack-strategy effects are conditioned on that specific third-party model and may shift with a different one; (5) scoring is deterministic regex/set-membership matching against structured ground truth and is blind to paraphrased disclosures, most acutely under P4 (abstracted disclosure) and P5 (conflicting objectives), meaning minimal-disclosure judgments are normative rather than empirically calibrated against human raters; (6) the capability-correlation analysis is limited to a single external benchmark (GPQA Diamond) due to limited availability of consistently reported external scores across the evaluated model set; (7) the defense-baseline evaluation (PrivacyChecker) is applied to only 3 of the 22 trusted models, with broader defense evaluation left to future work.

datasets_used: POLAR-Bench: 7,852 samples across 10 domains (medical, recruitment, finance, education, customer support, legal, insurance, housing, travel, cybersecurity), 733-1000 samples per domain, synthetically generated via symbolic profile sampling + LLM rendering, quality-controlled via regex attribute-coverage checks and LLM-judge attacker-prompt validation
quantitative_results: Overall score (λ=0.5) ranking: GLM-5.1 94.6, GPT-5.4 92.6, Gemma-4-31B 91.4, DeepSeek-V3.1 91.0, Gemma-4-E4B 90.9, ... GLM-4.7-Flash 80.1, lowest scorers SmolLM3-3B and Apertus models
GPQA Diamond vs. Privacy correlation: Pearson r=0.752 [95% CI 0.500,0.903], Spearman ρ=0.848 [0.559,0.966], n=20, p<0.001
GPQA Diamond vs. Utility correlation: Pearson r=-0.291 [-0.648,0.083], Spearman ρ=-0.156 [-0.609,0.331], n=20 (CIs straddle zero)
GPQA Diamond vs. Overall correlation: Pearson r=0.579 [0.177,0.807], Spearman ρ=0.584 [0.126,0.859]

PrivacyChecker defense effect: Ministral-3-3B privacy 48.27→75.79 (+27.52), utility 82.30→79.58 (-2.72); Apertus-70B privacy 57.78→63.67 (+5.89), utility 82.35→83.89 (+1.54); SmolLM3-3B privacy 46.32→56.52 (+10.20), utility 78.10→78.50 (+0.40)

Third-party model (E) sensitivity: Llama-3.3-70B as T varies overall score by only 0.2pp across three E models (86.64, 86.50, 86.46 for E=GLM-4.7-Flash/Apertus-70B/Llama-3.3-70B); Apertus-70B as T shows a 3.9pp swing in privacy score (61.18, 57.78, 57.27) across the same three E models
Apertus family: privacy drops from 65.66 (8B) to 57.78 (70B) despite utility improving with scale
Ministral family privacy: 62.03 (3B) → 68.04 (8B) → 58.99 (14B), non-monotonic, utility nearly flat
Domain-level privacy: Recruitment lowest privacy mean 73.27 with highest privacy variance 601.65; Housing 88.46 and Travel 85.40 substantially higher privacy means
Domain-vs-overall ranking Spearman correlations range 0.893-0.982 across domains; Kendall's W=0.915
Kimi-K2.5 lowest privacy variance (0.03) and lowest utility variance (92.89) among all models, i.e. most stable across domains
Attacker-prompt validation acceptance rates across 3 judges (GPT-5.4-nano, Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct): 80.0%, 91.7%, 91.7%, mean 87.8%, std 5.5%
baselines_compared: 22 evaluated LLMs as trusted agent T: GPT-5.4, GLM-4.7-Flash, GLM-5.1, Kimi-K2.5, DeepSeek-V3.1, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, Gemma-3-27B, Gemma-4-E2B/E4B/31B, GPT-OSS-20B, GPT-OSS-120B, Apertus-8B-Instruct-2509, Apertus-70B-Instruct-2509, Ministral-3-3B/8B/14B, OLMo-3.1-32B, SmolLM3-3B, Qwen3-32B
PrivacyChecker (Wang et al. 2025) evaluated as a representative inference-time defense on top of Ministral-3-3B, Apertus-70B, and SmolLM3-3B
Prior benchmarks compared structurally in Table 1 (not run as numeric baselines on this dataset): AirGapAgent, ConfAIde, PAPILLON, PrivacyLens, AGENTDAM, AgentLeak
